speaker: interviewer
text: Manya, you were going to tell me about an incident which involved having a weapon, and why you needed to have weapons while you were in the forest. Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very important to have weapons, as I said. I'm repeating it that, first of all, with a weapon you could do-- you could obtain more weapons. You could also obtain more food. Don't forget it was getting towards winter. There were no food in the fields. And this is how it happened that there are quite a few Jewish guys and girls that got organized. They obtained weapons in exchange for clothing. They went to their places of work and places of where they lived, and they got clothing. And for the clothing, you know, the farmers, all of them had weapons. The majority of weapons were hunting rifles and revolvers, and stuff like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they obtained, they got organized, and there were about 11 of them, nine boys and two girls. And they went into a farmer's house, and they took away her sewing machine. That angered her a lot, and she decided she is going to do something about it. So she went to the [GERMAN] to the place of where the Germans were, you know, they were having their ruling place, you know? So she went there and she complained. She says that Jews are roaming the forest, and they are robbing and they are stealing. And why don't the Germans do something about it? And they did. They send out a couple Germans with a Ukrainian group, and they were chasing that particular group. And they caught up with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- one night, nine-- eight of them were sleeping in the barn, and the two girls with the boy were sleeping in the house. They caught them, and they did a horrible death to them. They cut off their ears. They cut out their tongues. They cut out their eyes, and they bound them to-- to the tails of horses and they dragged them through the villages to show the villagers of what's going to happen to them if they are going to assist Jews. At this point, it was-- it must've been October-- November of 1942-- partisan movements started getting organized. And I don't mean strictly from Jewish people. As a matter of fact, it started from escapees from the prisoner of wars camp. You know, an awful lot of soldiers got into the prisoners of war. And Hitler with his regime, treated them like animals, even worse than that. So they escaped, and they started organized group of partisans of-- to fight the front lines, to fight the Germans from the backs. And they appointed-- and they did it already from-- with the-- with the consent of the Politburo of the Russian Communist Party. They sent one of their guys. His name was Kovpac. He was a partisan in World War I. He was an older guy already, and he dealt with this kind of thing. And he organized a group from about 2,000, 3,000 people. And they were-- their activities were in that area where we were. And as I said, the majority of them were escapees. But it wasn't enough, so they went into villages and they plain mobilized the Ukrainian youth to tell them that, you know, this is how they're going to fight the Germans. At that point, we were in a desperate situation, meaning myself, my father, my sister, my brother, and also my cousin, with a friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: We did not know what's going to happen in the winter. The snow will fall. There will be the-- the steps. You know, you could see the-- the-- where the people are walking. And how are we going to go out from our place of hiding? So we built a little bunker underneath, and it was sort of, like, camouflaged, covered. But my fa-- in that area lived a group of Seven Day Adventists. They don't believe in fighting. They don't believe in killing. So my father secured for my sister and I a place they should keep us. And we would help them out with their house chores.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they did. They were very nice. And they tried to convert us to their religion, but I said no. I survived so far as a Jew. I'm going to go on. And we stayed with them for maybe two weeks. When that incident with the store, with the girls that I told you, happened, we-- we got terribly, terribly frightened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here they caught people with weapons and they killed them. What's going to happen if they are going to catch us and we don't have any weapon? So we wanted to-- to do one thing. We wanted to disguise me as a-- as a farmer. And I should go with one of the farmers.

speaker: interviewee
text: He consented to go to the city where we secure a box of clothing, like overcoats, and boots, and stuff like. And take this clothing and bring into the forest and exchange it for some rifles. And that was the plan. We were supposed to do it. In the meantime, this organization, this outfit, the Kovpac outfit, had sent out like a group of-- how should you say it-- research group of, looking for people to they should join-- join them. And when they came to that forest and they came across my father, my father was elated. He thought that this is the salvation. He knows we're not going to escape. We're not going to live through it. Sooner or later, we'll be killed. There's no way for us. Nobody's on our side. But to be in the partisans and to fight the Germans, this is a dream come true. So we joined them. And they took us in. We had to do some plenty work. They had-- they took away a herd of cattle. They had to have food, so they had to have cattle. So we were chasing the cattle to the place where they were staying. But we were ambushed by Germans and they took away their cattle. Nothing happened to us, and we finally joined the center of the activity of that particular group, of the partisans that were called Kovpac. When we came in, we couldn't-- we couldn't believe our eyes. It was like living in a-- in a-- in a country with no Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were about, already by that time, 5,000 people. So they divided themselves into five brigades. Each brigade counted 1,000 people. And they used to occupy villages at the same-- simultaneously, and stay. And what-- how-- how did they survive? As I said, they used to take away cattle from the farmers.

speaker: interviewee
text: They also did diversified activities by ambushing German soldiers that were traveling from city to city, from village to village. And trucks, they were ser-- traveling in huge groups. They-- they used to kill them, take away their weapons, take away their clothes, and being able to act further, they also were dynamiting bridges. They came to cities and-- and ambushed the whole center of their activities. And they killed the Germans, and they took away their food, like grains, and all kinds of food that you could store, and brought it into the forest. And this is how they-- they acted as a regular piece of army. And this is how they assisted the front lines in fighting the Germans, by doing damage in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your role? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so, since they said that they're taking anybody, girls, boys. So my sister and I joined, and they took into one brigade that dealt specifically with doing all the cooking and all the sewing and all the-- the man-- mending clothing, you know, for the-- for the soldiers. I mean, it was-- you dealt with 1,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were doing all these things. It was all [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like-- like living on a farm, cooking for them. You needed three meals a day. Then there were also-- while they were doing all their activities by you know, doing all the fighting with the Germans and killing, they had wounded. So they brought the wounded, and you had to tend to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was ser-- they put me in charge, like I was a nurse, and I was tending the wounded. And-- and in the meantime, because of the unsanitary conditions-- don't forget that we're living in the same clothes, no bath, no change, no nothing. We just slept in the same clothes. We slept in the forest or sometimes in a village. And the sanitary conditions were horrible. So we were afflicted with a tremendous amount of lice. And these lice were carrying diseases. And there-- there was an outbreak of typhoid fever. So I was-- I was tending to the sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meantime, my sin-- my sister was a seamstress. There was not much work for her, so they asked her where did she want to go? And she said, she would like to join her father. By the way, we were scattered. We were not in the same brigade. She would like to join where her father and her brother is. So they fulfilled her wish and they sent her there. And I was serving as a nurse. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It went on between-- between January up until March. We wear not stationed in the same place all the time. We were roaming, like, we were two days in one little forest, three days in a village, five days in another place.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were constantly on the move, constantly going. You know, you couldn't stay, because there was a-- a big army. By the same token, there was also a partisan movement that was organized like a stationary groups that stayed in the same place. They were small outfits, like 200 people. They were also-- they had a radius. They were assigned a-- assigned a radius like, about, let's say 20 kilometers. And this is where they were doing their work. They had to ambush Germans in this city, or go and take away some product from this city, or-- or do some dynamiting of-- of a particular house, or killing the leader of the Germans. This was their assignment. And we were sort of in the background, helping them out, the girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm wondering, how did you get, like, medical supplies? Could you get any?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a problem. There were some that they threw down at night in parachutes from Russia. But let me tell you of an-- what happened. And this is where I had a-- a part in it. That's how I survived. We were not far from a city called Baranowicze, B-A-R-A-N-O-W-I-C-Z-E. Not far from there was a little town, and next to that town was a huge [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lake. And that lake froze. And the winter of 1943 was extremely hard. It was an awful lot of below zero temperature.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they have-- at this point they had already accumulated a few hundred wounded people. What do you do with them? There were no hospitals. We had doctors. We didn't have too much medical supplies. We did the best we could. They used to ambush hospitals and take away some supplies, but it wasn't enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, they were running out of ammunition. They didn't have enough rifles. They didn't have enough dynamite. They didn't have enough of a lot of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they decided to build, like, a temporary airfield and bring airplanes at night, without light, that were landing. For an entire month, every night, they had airplanes landing on that lake that served as a airfield. And they were bringing in supplies.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were bringing in clothes, and-- and medical supplies, and all kinds of weapons, and all kind of dynamite. And they were taking away the wounded, which was more important than that. And when-- when the month-- towards the end of the month, the last plane that landed, it was already sank in the water, because the-- the-- the ice couldn't hold out. And this is how the Germans got a hold of the situation. They learned about it, and they started pursuing them. They saw that this was a tremendous hindrance to their war. In-- in that-- in that period of time and at that month, I was sick with typhoid, and I was burning up with fever for about 10 days. I was lying in a little room with about 18 sick. Every night somebody else died. I didn't have no medi-- no-- not one pill, no medical assistance. I hardly had a little water to drink. I used to lie there for hours and just scream, water. I was burned up. But miraculously, I-- my crisis passed and I started recuperating. When I started recuperating was just at the end of that month when they couldn't land anymore planes. And one-- so they-- as-- as I started recuperating, they shaved my head. I weighed maybe 80 pounds. They-- they deloused me, so to speak. And they said, enough. Enough being sick. Now you're able and you're immune to the sickness. Come back to work. So they assigned me another sick person with a driver and a horse and buggy, in case we have to go, they could-- they should be able to drive her. At that point, we stayed in a village. And that was one night we heard a knock on the window and we said--

speaker: interviewer
text: What village was it, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was near Pinsk. It was called Berenziki, b-E-R-E-N-Z-I-K-I. Near Pinsk, Pinsk spelled P-I-N-S-K. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes, we were told that we were surrounded. The Germans took off a bunch of-- 45,000 Germans were chasing that partisan group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were told that we have to go. We can't stay in that place anymore. So the driver yells-- and by the way, at that point, I was so weak that I couldn't walk on my feet. I couldn't drag my feet at all. And I lost my hearing capacity. And also, something happened to my brain. I didn't function. I couldn't function, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because I was so sick. So he started yelling at me, and telling me that we have to go. We had to put the-- the sick woman down and-- and we have to put her in the-- put her in the-- the buggy. I said, I can't do. I couldn't move. I just couldn't move at all. So, he was a very nice guy. He put her up, and he did all the work. And he said, OK, now you get dressed. And I'm going to take you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, you can do what you want, I'm not going. Because I-- I lost my mind. I didn't know what was going on. If I would have known that the Germans are chasing us and I'm going to fall into the Germans, and I'm Jewish and they're going to kill me, would I have said I'm not going? So he went and he called the-- the doctor and he called the head of the group. And he-- they came and they told me that they-- they have to go. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] So I said-- so I said, I'm not going. And they came and they said, you don't understand. You're going to be killed by the German. I said, I don't care. I-- see, I wasn't-- my mind wasn't working. And they left. They thought, what difference does it make? Another person dead. And they left. They couldn't force me. They couldn't drag me. And I was-- I left in that house. I stayed in that house. When I got up, they left. And all of a sudden, it was quiet. There was nobody there. I-- I remained in that house. And at night, I remember, I told you that there were local partisans that lived in that area. They came and they said to me, oh, my god, you know what happened to you? The Germans are going to kill you. So I said, so why don't you take me with you? They said, no, we can't. And they-- they just left, and they-- they-- they didn't say anything. And I stayed in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: That night-- when I got up in the morning, the village was completely empty because the Germans were telling-- because they assisted the partisans, they were telling the villagers that they should-- they-- that they assisted the villagers. And the villagers were afraid, since they assisted them, that the Germans were going to kill them, so they escaped in the forest, too. I am roaming the village. There was nobody there. So I went back to the house. I got on the-- on the Russian oven, and I was lying there. And the same day, about 1:00, I take a look through the window. The-- the village is full of tanks with Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I couldn't differentiate, because when the partisans were killing Germans, they were taking off their clothes and they were wearing their coats. So I didn't-- I didn't understand what's going on. Are they partisans or Germans? But I didn't say. I just-- so I run out in the-- and I grabbed a ladder, and I climbed up in the attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came in and they were shooting all over the place. I was looking out and I was getting terribly cold. I was sick. I was 80 pounds. I didn't have a blood-- a drop of blood in me. So I thought to myself, I don't care what's going to happen. I really didn't care if I was dying or living. And I went down and I stayed in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Germans came in, and they were flabbergasted. What are you doing here? So subconsciously, I understood. I cannot understand German. I can't-- I don't understand a word they're saying. That's what I told myself. So I spoke in Polish and I said that I am-- I'm Polish and I'm sick. And so they said, well, they're moving into that village and they have to-- they have to stay here overnight. I should clean up. They said, how come there's so much straw-- partisans. Were there partisans here? I said, I know nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, one German started bothering me terribly. Said to me come here. You come and show me where you hid your silver and your jewelry. And I said, I don't know what you're talking about. He was ready to shoot me. And at that moment, they got a-- an order that they can-- they have to move on further. You know, they were settled in the village. And they moved on. And as they were moving on, there were horse and buggies. They were moving on. And all of a sudden, they told them to stop. And each wagon was getting into another-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] towards another house. And another group came in. And as they came in, the old-- same old story, with the rifle towards me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, I'm Polish. I don't understand. So they said, OK. You get up and help us. And you're going to cook dinner for us. So I did. I made a fire and I made scrambled eggs for them and they had hard cheeses and salamis. And they sat down and we ate. And they spoke between themselves. And they said, what do you think she is, Polish or Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: And one guy said, I really don't know. And as we finished eating, he said, you can't sleep in this house. And two of them took one rifle in the front and one in the back, and they're leading me out the door. And I thought, uh oh, they're probably going to shoot me. But I'm not-- I-- it doesn't bother me. I mean, I don't care what they're going to do to me. So I-- they put me into another house. Now, what happened in that village that night, as the partisans left, they were mining all the roads. And as they were the mining the roads and the Germans came in, so they had a lot of casualties.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had to land their own planes to take away their casualties. In that min-- in-- in-- in all-- and they were putting up flares. They were very scared, the Germans. They were constantly shooting. I went into that house and as the-- the-- the noise was coming on, I fell asleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I woke up in the morning, and there was not one German. Everybody left. There was nobody in the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meantime, as they were leaving, they came across these farmers that were in the forest. And they said to them, why did you run away from your village? We are not harming the tot-- the local population. We're only after partisans. You go back. So they started coming back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as they came back, the woman of that house came back. And she had a little boy. And she said to me, they thought that the front line is getting closer and that the Russians will be occupying. So she-- and the Germans are escaping. That's what they thought is happening. They didn't know that the-- these Germans are chasing partisans. Anyway, so she said to me, you know what, I-- my husband is in the army. I am here with a little boy. You're going to stay with me. Whatever will happen to us will happen to you. And-- and we're going to take care of one another. And we started cooking dinner, and I put some potatoes in the Russian oven. And I took the utensils, and I was started taking out the potatoes. And all of a sudden I felt like steam is coming out of my brain. And all of a sudden, the picture became completely clear to me that I was in the partisans, and I was-- there I had my father, my sister, my brother, although we were not together. And now, I am-- I am done with my life. I am now-- if I'm not going to kill-- be killed by the Germans, I'll be killed by the farmers here. And they're against the Jews, and they know-- they knew me around there, you know, that-- I-- because a farmer was taking care of me a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, so-- so I said to her, I started crying hysterically. And I got a terrible, terrible headache. And I said to her, you know, you don't understand. I am not a sick person. I had-- I explained to her. She looked at me and she said, why-- did you have these attacks at home, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: She thought that I'm completely out of my mind, that I'm crazy. Nevertheless, she said, oh, she didn't want to talk to me. I'm-- I'm nobody. I'm a crazy person. And we went to sleep. And we lie down on the oven because it was warm there. And I made a plan that, no matter what, I'm going-- not tonight because I felt very sick-- I'm going to go into-- going to the forest. And-- and in the forest, sort of, I was safe. You know, the forest was like my home. I was hiding behind the trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but it must've been about 4:00 in the morning. It was still dark outside. We heard terrible noise. And as we looked out through the window, the village was full of German tanks, with Germans, with horses, with everything. I thought to myself, now that I know my score, what am I going to do? So I said to her, why don't we change clothes? You give me your clothes and I leave you my clothes. She says, if you say one more word I'm going out in the street and I'm going to tell the German that you're a Jew. So I said, OK. In meantime, a German came into the house, and he asked her for some food for the horse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as she walked out to give it to him, she walked out in the street to take a look what's going on. The forest was like from here across the street. I picked myself up, and I ran into the forest. OK. Let me tell him because he doesn't--
